abstract:
While the account of Jesus’ mission in Judea (Jn 3:22–36) is well-known, its specific geographical setting has received surprisingly little scholarly attention. Closely tied to this oversight is the identity of the "Jew" (Ἰουδαῖος) mentioned in John 3:25, who is depicted in an argument with the disciples of John the Baptist. This paper argues that analyzing the broader geographical structure of Jesus’ ministry from John 2:13 to 4:54 offers a fresh perspective on these problems. 

The Fourth Gospel appears to organize Jesus’ mission according to a geographical progression similar to the apostolic mandate in Acts 1:8—moving from Jerusalem through Judea and Samaria—though John concludes this specific sequence in Galilee. This trajectory ensures that all of Israel has the opportunity to hear his message and come to faith before the onset of the intense legal and theological confrontations with the leadership in chapters 5 through 10. By situating the Judean mission within this systematic outreach to the whole of Israel, the term Ἰουδαῖος in John 3:25 is best understood not as a generic religious label, but as a specific regional designation: a "Judean."
